_meta.purpose: Curated bedrock list of characteristic Solent saltmarsh / mudflat / intertidal ANIMALS — independent of iNaturalist coverage. Parallel to data/saltmarsh_bedrock.json (plants). These are species that the saltmarsh ecosystem is built around but that show up sparsely or not at all in iNat's casual-observer record (moths flown at night, marine inverts, hidden specialists).
references: Doody, J.P. (2008) Saltmarsh Conservation, Management and Restoration. Springer.
JNCC UK BAP Priority Habitat: Coastal Saltmarsh — https://jncc.gov.uk/our-work/uk-bap-priority-habitats/
_meta.monthsAre: 1=January .. 12=December. 'active' = months when the species is on the wing / above the mud / visible to a kayaker.
_meta.ecologyZones: saltmarsh-surface (between and on the plants), mudflat (bare drying mud at low tide), creek-channel (the running water), strandline (drift line above HW), intertidal-hard (rocks/pilings/breakwaters), open-water (Solent itself)
_meta.taxonGroupNote: iNat's 'iconicTaxon' lumps non-bird/non-mammal/non-plant animals under 'Animalia'; we keep a 'taxonGroup' field for the actual lineage (Annelida, Crustacea, Cnidaria, etc.) so the picker can talk about a worm/crab/jelly correctly.

species.characteristicOf: upper saltmarsh and coastal dune grasses
notes: Scarce specialist whose caterpillars feed inside the stems of Sea Couch and other saltmarsh grasses. Adults fly July to September and come to light — the saltmarsh moth most worth a midsummer trap on the upper marsh.
Tiny T-shaped plume moth whose caterpillars feed only on Sea Lavender — the moth's flight peaks while the lavender is in flower. A walking match to the host.
Coastal-saltmarsh moth whose stripey caterpillars spin a communal silk tent in spring and feed on saltmarsh and strandline plants — the adult flies July to August.

species.characteristicOf: Sea Aster flowers; nests in soft sandy ground above HW
notes: Late-season specialist that emerges only as the Sea Aster comes into flower. Nests in soft sandy ground above the high-tide line and is still on the wing into October when most other bees have gone.

species.characteristicOf: sun-warmed paths and walls along the upper marsh and strandline
notes: Coastal grassland butterfly in two broods — April-to-June and August-to-September — basking with wings half open on hard substrate. In national decline but holding on along the Solent's warm coastal strip.

species.characteristicOf: bare drying mud across the whole creek
notes: Tiny brown snails by the hundred thousand per square metre on the bare mud — the foundational food source for Shelduck, Wigeon, and the wintering waders. Older books and most older records use the genus name Hydrobia.
Marks itself for you twice a tide: a spiralled cast of digested sand at one end of the U-shaped burrow, and a small depression where the worm draws breath at the other.

species.characteristicOf: soft creek mud, throughout the inter-tidal
notes: Iridescent green-bronze segmented worm in soft mud — the staple of Curlew, Redshank and the godwits. Most of Ashlett's winter wader population is ultimately a ragworm forecast.
Filter-feeds buried in the upper mud — siphon-pairs leave faint stars on the surface as the tide returns. Empty shells form the white sand of the strandline above HW.

species.characteristicOf: creek mud at low water; under wrack at the strandline
notes: Ubiquitous in the creek and at low water, dark green to brown. The pale-bellied juveniles and moulting females hide under tide-cast wrack at the strandline. iNat records this as 'European Green Crab' (it is invasive in the Americas, native here).

species.characteristicOf: upper strandline, sea walls, breakwaters at and above HW
notes: Big nocturnal isopod of the upper strandline — sometimes called the rock slater. Walk a Solent tideline at dusk and they will run over your boots.

species.characteristicOf: rocks, breakwaters and pier pilings at the lower edge of the marsh
notes: Plain dark snail of rocks, breakwaters and pier-pilings — eaten in Britain since Roman times. The Solent population is at the southern edge of the species' UK abundance peak.

species.characteristicOf: the channel in the Solent itself
notes: Brown radial 'compass' markings on a pale bell, working the Solent channel from June through September. Usually well below the swim from a kayak, but stranded in numbers along the strandline after onshore winds.

species.characteristicOf: Solent open water and Calshot Spit haul-outs
notes: Resident in the Solent — most often hauled out on the eastern bar at Calshot, or sleeping in the shingle south of the spit. A V-shaped pair of nostrils and a 'dog-faced' profile distinguishes Phoca from the Roman-nosed Grey Seal.
